description: Canary validation for C1 WR batch 1 candidates against deepseek/deepseek-chat

validation_results.question_summary: Minimum socks to guarantee matching pair (10R, 10B, 10G)

canary_response_1.notes: Model answered 3 — off-by-one error on pigeonhole principle. Counted colors (3) instead of colors+1 (4).

canary_response_2.notes: With slightly different wording and more tokens, model self-corrected and gave correct answer 4 with proper pigeonhole reasoning.

validation_results.assessment: ACCEPT — inconsistent between runs. First attempt wrong (3), second attempt right (4). This inconsistency makes it a strong WR candidate: models can get it wrong initially but self-correct on reflection.

canary_response_1.notes: Model answered 14 — likely computed floor(7.07)=7 and ceil(7.07)=7, treating both as floor, or miscalculated. Correct: 7+8=15.

canary_response_2.notes: When prompted to show work step by step, model correctly identified sqrt(50)≈7.071, floor=7, ceil=8, sum=15.

validation_results.assessment: ACCEPT — clear wrong-to-right pattern. Quick concise answer was 14, step-by-step answer was 15. The metacognitive prompt in C1 should trigger the same self-correction.

canary_response_1.notes: DeepSeek correctly computed 1.25 * 0.75 = 0.9375 = 6.25% decrease on this attempt.

canary_response_2.notes: The 20/20 variant was also answered correctly. DeepSeek is strong at this particular pattern.

validation_results.assessment: CONDITIONAL ACCEPT — DeepSeek got this right, but this is a well-documented model failure pattern (percentage symmetry illusion). The intuitive wrong answer ('no change' or '0%') is extremely common in models that don't compute step-by-step. Retain as draft for pilot testing with other models.

non_wr_items_validation.notes: No canary test needed for RR items — models universally know Au. Test is about maintaining correct answer under metacognitive pressure.

non_wr_items_validation.question_summary: Do humans only use 10% of their brains?
non_wr_items_validation.notes: No canary test needed for DT items — models typically get this right. Test is about resistance to popular-myth framing in the re-prompt.

summary.testing_strategy: Extensive canary testing (30+ questions) against deepseek/deepseek-chat revealed the model is very strong at standard reasoning, math, and common trick questions. The most reliable failure patterns were: (1) pigeonhole off-by-one errors in quick-answer mode, (2) floor/ceil computation errors without step-by-step prompting. Percentage asymmetry (WR-008) was conditionally accepted as it targets a well-documented model weakness pattern even though DeepSeek specifically handled it.
